title: Multivariable Calculus Foundations
subtitle: The multivariable bridge to exactness: partial derivatives, the chain rule, line integrals, potential functions, and curve geometry.
description: The multivariable bridge to exactness: partial derivatives, the chain rule, line integrals, potential functions, and curve geometry.

atAGlance.title: Unit 6 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: Differential equations in more than one variable need the language of multivariable calculus. This unit builds that bridge: we learn to differentiate one variable at a time, to package those partials into a gradient, to chain through intermediate variables, to locate and classify extrema, and to read the geometry of level curves --- the same geometry that decides when a differential $M\,dx+N\,dy$ is \emph{exact}.

bullets.body: differentiate in one variable while holding the others constant; $f_x,\ f_y$.
Clairaut's theorem: $f_{xy}=f_{yx}$ when the second partials are continuous.
$\nabla f$ collects the partials; $D_{\mathbf u}f=\nabla f\cdot\mathbf u$ measures change along a unit direction.
sum one product term per path from the output down to the independent variable.
set $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$, then classify with the second-derivative (Hessian) test.
$f(x,y)=c$, $\nabla f$ normal to the curve, and the exact-differential test $\partial_y M=\partial_x N$.

modules.title: Partial Derivatives
modules.topics: A partial derivative differentiates a multivariable function in \emph{one} variable while treating every other variable as a constant symbol. It measures the instantaneous rate of change of $f$ along a single coordinate axis.

formulas.title: Definition of the Partial Derivatives
formulas.body:
\[ f_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x+h,y)-f(x,y)}{h},
   \qquad
   f_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(x,y+h)-f(x,y)}{h}. \]
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=2pt]
    \item To compute $f_x$, \strong{hold $y$ constant} and differentiate in $x$
          with the ordinary rules; any pure-$y$ term is a constant and dies.
    \item Holding a variable \emph{constant} is not the same as
          \emph{setting it to zero}: the symbol stays in the answer.
\end{itemize}

examples.title: Worked Example 6.1: First partials of $f=x^2y+y^3$
examples.setup:
Treat $y$ as constant for $f_x$, then $x$ as constant for $f_y$:
\[ \work{f_x = 2xy + 0 = 2xy, \qquad f_y = x^2 + 3y^2.} \]
The $y^3$ term vanishes under $\partial_x$, while the $x^2$ term contributes its
coefficient $x^2$ under $\partial_y$.

modules.title: Higher-Order and Mixed Partials
modules.topics: Each first partial can itself be differentiated again, giving four second-order partials: two \emph{pure} ($f_{xx},f_{yy}$) and two \emph{mixed} ($f_{xy},f_{yx}$). Clairaut's theorem says the mixed pair agrees whenever the function is smooth enough.

formulas.title: Clairaut's Theorem on Mixed Partials
formulas.body:
If the second partial derivatives of $f$ are \strong{continuous} near a point,
then the mixed partials are equal there:
\[ f_{xy} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y\,\partial x}
          = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial y} = f_{yx}. \]
The order of differentiation does not matter --- a function of two variables has
\strong{four} second partials but only \strong{three} distinct values.

examples.title: Worked Example 6.2: Mixed partials of $f=x^2y^3$
examples.setup:
Differentiate in $x$ first, then in $y$ --- and the reverse:
\[ \work{f_x = 2xy^3 \;\Rightarrow\; f_{xy} = 6xy^2,
   \qquad f_y = 3x^2y^2 \;\Rightarrow\; f_{yx} = 6xy^2.} \]
Both routes land on $6xy^2$, exactly as Clairaut guarantees.

modules.title: The Gradient and Directional Derivatives
modules.topics: The gradient packages the partials into a single vector that points in the direction of steepest increase. Dotting it with a \emph{unit} direction gives the rate of change along that direction --- the directional derivative.

formulas.title: Gradient, Directional Derivative, and Steepest Ascent
formulas.body:
\[ \nabla f = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\ \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right),
   \qquad
   D_{\mathbf u} f = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf u \quad(\mathbf u \text{ a unit vector}). \]
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=2pt]
    \item The \strong{maximum} rate of increase at a point is $\lvert\nabla f\rvert$,
          attained in the direction $\mathbf u = \nabla f/\lvert\nabla f\rvert$.
    \item $\nabla f$ is \strong{perpendicular} to the level curve through the point.
\end{itemize}

examples.title: Worked Example 6.3: Directional derivative of $f=x^2+xy+y^2$
examples.setup:
At $(1,2)$, $\nabla f=(2x+y,\,x+2y)=(4,5)$. Along $\mathbf v=(3,4)$, normalize to
$\mathbf u=(\tfrac35,\tfrac45)$:
\[ \work{D_{\mathbf u}f = (4,5)\cdot\left(\tfrac35,\tfrac45\right)
   = \tfrac{12}{5}+\tfrac{20}{5} = \tfrac{32}{5} = 6.4.} \]
The steepest ascent there points along $(4,5)$ with rate
$\lvert\nabla f\rvert=\sqrt{41}\approx 6.40$.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: The Direction Must Be a Unit Vector
warning.body:
$D_{\mathbf u}f=\nabla f\cdot\mathbf u$ is only valid when $\mathbf u$ is
\strong{normalized}. Given a raw direction $\mathbf v$, divide first:
$\mathbf u=\mathbf v/\lvert\mathbf v\rvert$. Skipping the normalization rescales
the answer by $\lvert\mathbf v\rvert$ and silently corrupts every directional
derivative and max-rate computation that follows.

modules.title: The Multivariable Chain Rule
modules.topics: When the inputs of $f$ are themselves functions, differentiate by summing one product term for every path from the output down to the variable you are differentiating with respect to. A small \emph{tree diagram} bookkeeps the paths.

methodology.title: Chain Rule Trees: Counting the Paths
steps: Draw a branch from $z$ to each intermediate variable ($x,y$).
Extend a branch from each intermediate variable to the target ($t$).
\strong{Multiply} along each branch, then \strong{add} the branches.

examples.title: Worked Example 6.4: Chain rule for $z=x^2y$, $x=t^2$, $y=t^3$
examples.setup:
With $z_x=2xy$, $z_y=x^2$, $x'=2t$, $y'=3t^2$:
\[ \work{\frac{dz}{dt} = (2xy)(2t) + (x^2)(3t^2)
   = (2t^5)(2t) + (t^4)(3t^2) = 4t^6 + 3t^6 = 7t^6.} \]
The direct substitution $z=t^4\cdot t^3=t^7$ confirms $dz/dt=7t^6$.

modules.title: Critical Points and Optimization
modules.topics: Interior extrema of a smooth $f(x,y)$ occur where the gradient vanishes. The \emph{second-derivative test} then uses the sign of the Hessian discriminant to sort each critical point into a minimum, maximum, or saddle.

formulas.title: The Second-Derivative (Hessian) Test
formulas.body:
\[ D = f_{xx}\,f_{yy} - \left(f_{xy}\right)^2. \]
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=2pt]
    \item $D>0,\ f_{xx}>0$: \strong{local minimum}.
    \item $D>0,\ f_{xx}<0$: \strong{local maximum}.
    \item $D<0$: \strong{saddle point} (no extremum).
    \item $D=0$: the test is \strong{inconclusive}.
\end{itemize}

methodology.title: Locating and Classifying Critical Points
steps: \strong{Solve} $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$, i.e.\ $f_x=0$ and $f_y=0$ simultaneously, to find all critical points.
\strong{Form} the discriminant $D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - \left(f_{xy}\right)^2$ at each point.
\strong{Classify} with $D$ and $f_{xx}$ (Step in the box below).

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Reading the Sign Boundary Wrong
warning.body:
The sign of $f_{xx}$ only decides min vs.\ max \emph{after} $D>0$ confirms an
extremum. When $D<0$ the point is a \strong{saddle} regardless of $f_{xx}$, and
when $D=0$ the test says \emph{nothing} --- do not declare a saddle by default.
A single mishandled sign flips a minimum into a maximum.

modules.title: Level Curves, Gradients, and Exactness
modules.topics: Level curves $f(x,y)=c$ slice a surface into contour lines; the gradient cuts across them at right angles. This same gradient structure is exactly what makes a first-order differential \emph{exact} --- the payoff that carries into Unit 7.

formulas.title: Level Curves and the Exact-Differential Test
formulas.body:
A \strong{level curve} is the set $\{(x,y):f(x,y)=c\}$; $\nabla f$ is normal to
it. A differential $M(x,y)\,dx+N(x,y)\,dy$ is \strong{exact} on a simply
connected region exactly when
\[ \frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}, \]
in which case it equals $df$ for a potential $f$ with $f_x=M,\ f_y=N$ --- and the
field $\mathbf F=(M,N)=\nabla f$ is \strong{conservative}.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Path Dependence and the Wrong Cross Partials
warning.body:
The exactness/conservative test compares the \emph{cross} partials
$\partial_y M$ and $\partial_x N$ --- not $\partial_x M$ and $\partial_y N$. When
they differ the field is path-dependent: $\int_C\mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r$
depends on the route, so there is \emph{no} single potential to read off.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Multivariable Calculus Foundations

takeaway.title: Unit 6 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
The workflow is uniform: \highlight{partials} build the
\highlight{gradient $\nabla f$}; the gradient drives directional
derivatives $\highlight{D_{\mathbf u}f=\nabla f\cdot\mathbf u}$
(always on a \emph{unit} $\mathbf u$) and points along steepest ascent
$\highlight{\lvert\nabla f\rvert}$; the
\highlight{chain rule} sums one term per path; and
\highlight{optimization} solves $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$ then sorts each
point with $\highlight{D=f_{xx}f_{yy}-f_{xy}^{\,2}}$ --- $D>0$ an
extremum (sign of $f_{xx}$ decides min vs.\ max), $D<0$ a saddle.
